channel.title: quantum on Lily Wasko
channel.description: Recent content in quantum on Lily Wasko

item.title: Progress Report 01 - 10/25/2021
item.description: Slight update to research plan based on research paper N-qubit quantum teleportation, information splitting and superdense coding through the composite GHZ–Bell channel by Debashis Saha and Prasanta K. Panigrahi: Since N+1 qubits must be acted on in order to encode 2N + 1 classical bits, the pure GHZ 5 state is useless for superdense coding, and is being abandoned for the secure communication demo in favor of the composite GHZ-Bell state outlined in the research above.

item.title: Relevant details from 'N-qubit ... superdense coding through the composite GHZ–Bell channel'
item.description: This source is relevant to Secure Communication with GHZ 5.

item.title: Relevant details from Brilliant
item.description: This source is relevant to Secure Communication with GHZ 5.

item.title: Secure Communication Using Superdense Coding of the GHZ 5 State
item.description: View this project’s code on GitHub :) Background Quantum Computing Qubits The most basic unit of information in classical computing is the bit. It stores a value either of 0 or 1, which represent the presense or absense of electrons in a transistor. In quantum computing, however, the most basic unit of information is the qubit. A qubit, like a bit, can store either 0 or 1, but it also has the unique ability to store a value that is both 0 and 1: a superposition state.

item.title: Melanoma Detection with Quantum Convolutional Neural Networks
item.description: View this project’s code on GitHub :) Background Melanoma Melanoma is a deadly skin cancer. Out of all skin lesions,it is especially dangerous because it is outnumbered by other skin lesions, and it is difficult to identify. However, melanoma is best treated when caught early, so treatment relies on rapid identification. According to the Skin Cancer Foundation, an estimated 7,180 people will die from melanoma in 2021 alone.
